title.raw: 卷九十 酷吏傳 Volume 90: Cruel Officials

sentences.zh: 卷九十 酷吏傳第六十

translations.literal: Volume 90: Biographies of Cruel Officials, Number 60.
translations.idiomatic: Volume 90: Biographies of Harsh Officials (60).

sentences.zh: 孔子曰：「導之以政，齊之以刑，民免而無恥；

translations.literal: Confucius said: "Lead them with administration, align them with punishments—the people escape but lack shame;"
translations.idiomatic: Confucius said, "Guide them with edicts and police them with penalties, and they stay out of trouble but feel no shame;"

sentences.zh: 導之以德，齊之以禮，有恥且格。

translations.literal: lead them with virtue, align them with ritual—they have shame and moreover correct themselves."
translations.idiomatic: guide them with virtue and shape them with ritual, and they acquire a sense of shame and reform themselves."

sentences.zh: 」老氏稱：「上德不德，是以有德；

translations.literal: "The Old Master declared: "Highest virtue does not deem itself virtue—thereby it has virtue;"
translations.idiomatic: Laozi wrote, "The highest good does not advertise its goodness; that is why it truly is good;"

sentences.zh: 下德不失德，是以無德。

translations.literal: lower virtue does not lose virtue—thereby it lacks virtue."
translations.idiomatic: lesser goodness clings to being 'good,' and so achieves no real goodness."

sentences.zh: 法令滋章，盜賊多有。

translations.literal: Statutes and commands proliferate manifestly; robbers-thieves greatly increase.
translations.idiomatic: The more elaborate the laws, the more thieves and bandits appear.

sentences.zh: 」信哉是言也！

translations.literal: "How trustworthy are these words!"
translations.idiomatic: How true those words ring!"

sentences.zh: 法令者，治之具，而非制治清濁之原也。

translations.literal: Statutes and commands are tools of governance, yet they are not the root that regulates governance's clarity or turbidity.
translations.idiomatic: Laws are instruments of rule, not the source that decides whether government turns fair or foul.

sentences.zh: 昔天下之罔嘗密矣，然奸軌愈起，其極也，上下相遁，至於不振。

translations.literal: Formerly all-under-heaven's nets were once dense; yet wickedness surged more—their extreme: high and low mutually evaded, reaching non-recovery.
translations.idiomatic: Once the empire's legal nets grew ever tighter, crime only spread until rulers and ruled dodged one another and authority collapsed.

sentences.zh: 當是之時，吏治若救火揚沸，非武健嚴酷，惡能勝其任而愉快乎？

translations.literal: At this time, clerk governance was like fighting fire while ladling boiling water—unless martial, robust, stern, and cruel, how could they overcome their tasks and find satisfaction?
translations.idiomatic: Officialdom then was like fighting a blaze by scooping boiling broth—without brute severity, who could master the job or sleep easy?

sentences.zh: 言道德者，溺於職矣。

translations.literal: Those who spoke of morality drowned in office.
translations.idiomatic: Men who preached virtue drowned in the workload.

sentences.zh: 故曰：「聽訟吾猶人也，必也使無訟乎！

translations.literal: Therefore it is said: "In hearing lawsuits I am like other men—but surely there must be making no lawsuits!"
translations.idiomatic: Hence the saying: "In hearing cases I am no different from anyone else; what I want is to leave nothing to litigate."

sentences.zh: 」「下士聞道大笑之。

translations.literal: "The lowest gentlemen hear the Way and laugh greatly at it."
translations.idiomatic: "When small-minded men hear the Way, they burst out laughing."

sentences.zh: 」非虛言也。

translations.literal: "It is not empty speech.
translations.idiomatic: That was no idle remark."

sentences.zh: 漢興，破觚而為圜，斫雕而為樸，號為罔漏吞舟之魚。

translations.literal: When Han arose, they broke the square goblet and made it round, chopped carving and made plainness; it was called a net that did not leak fish that swallow boats.
translations.idiomatic: At Han's founding the state filed away Qin's sharp corners into smooth curves and stripped ornament back to plain wood—netting loose enough, it was said, that even a boat-swallowing fish could slip through.

sentences.zh: 而吏治蒸蒸，不至於奸，黎民艾安。

translations.literal: Yet clerk governance steamed upward, did not reach wickedness; black-haired people were pacified secure.
translations.idiomatic: Yet governance warmed and improved, crime never ran wild, and common folk lived untroubled.

sentences.zh: 由是觀之，在彼不在此。

translations.literal: Viewing from this, it lies in that, not in this.
translations.idiomatic: The lesson is that outcomes hinge on the spirit behind rule, not on the letter of the code alone.

sentences.zh: 高後時，酷吏獨有侯封，刻轢宗室，侵辱功臣。

translations.literal: In Empress Gao's time, among cruel clerks there was only Hou Feng; he carved and crushed the imperial clan and encroached on and humiliated merit ministers.
translations.idiomatic: Under the Grand Empress Dowager Lü the notable harsh official was Hou Feng, who rode roughshod over the imperial house and insulted the founders.

sentences.zh: 呂氏已敗，遂夷侯封之家。

translations.literal: After the Lü clan had been defeated, thereupon extirpated Hou Feng's household.
translations.idiomatic: When the Lü faction fell, Hou Feng's entire line was wiped out.

sentences.zh: 孝景時，晁錯以刻深頗用術輔其資，而七國之亂髮怒於錯，錯卒被戮。

translations.literal: In Emperor Jing's time, Chao Cuo by carving depth largely employed craft to assist his endowment; the Seven Kingdoms' rebellion flared anger at Cuo; Cuo finally met execution.
translations.idiomatic: Under Emperor Jing, Chao Cuo won influence through pitiless legalism; the Rebellion of the Seven States vented its rage on him, and he died under the blade.

sentences.zh: 其後有郅都、甯成之倫。

translations.literal: Afterward there were the likes of Zhi Du and Ning Cheng.
translations.idiomatic: Men such as Zhi Du and Ning Cheng followed.

sentences.zh: 郅都，河東大陽人也。

translations.literal: Zhi Du was a man of Dayang in Hedong.
translations.idiomatic: Zhi Du came from Dayang in Hedong Commandery.

sentences.zh: 以郎事文帝。

translations.literal: As a gentleman he served Emperor Wen.
translations.idiomatic: He served Emperor Wen in the corps of gentlemen.

sentences.zh: 景帝時為中郎將，敢直諫，面折大臣於朝。

translations.literal: In Emperor Jing's time he was leader of gentleman cadets—dared straight remonstrance, face rebuked great ministers at court.
translations.idiomatic: Under Emperor Jing he commanded the gentleman cadets, spoke blunt truth, and openly dressed down senior ministers in open court.

sentences.zh: 嘗從入上林，賈姬在廁，野彘入廁。

translations.literal: Once following him into Shanglin, Concubine Jia was in the privy; a wild boar entered the privy.
translations.idiomatic: On a hunt in Shanglin Park, a favorite consort named Jia withdrew to a latrine just as a wild boar burst in.

sentences.zh: 上目都，都不行。

translations.literal: The sovereign signaled Du; Du did not move.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor motioned Zhi Du to intervene; Zhi Du stood fast.

sentences.zh: 上欲自持兵救賈姬，都伏上前曰：「亡一姬復一姬進，天下所少寧姬等邪？

translations.literal: The sovereign wished himself to take weapons and save Concubine Jia; Du threw himself forward saying: "Lose one concubine and another concubine advances—what the realm lacks, is it surely concubines like this?"
translations.idiomatic: When the emperor reached for arms to rescue her, Zhi Du blocked him: "Lose one favorite and another takes her place—is the empire short of such women?"

sentences.zh: 陛下縱自輕，奈宗廟太后何？

translations.literal: Your Majesty even if slight yourself—how about the ancestral temples and the empress dowager?"
translations.idiomatic: Even if you discount your own safety, what of the altars of state and the empress dowager?"

sentences.zh: 」上還，彘亦不傷賈姬。

translations.literal: "The sovereign withdrew; the boar also did not harm Concubine Jia.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor held back; the boar left without harming Lady Jia.

sentences.zh: 太后聞之，賜都金百斤，上亦賜金百斤，由此重都。

translations.literal: The empress dowager heard it and granted Du a hundred jin of gold; the sovereign also granted a hundred jin of gold—thereby valued Du.
translations.idiomatic: The dowager rewarded Zhi Du with a hundred catties of gold; the emperor matched it, and both sides henceforth prized him.

sentences.zh: 濟南瞷氏宗人三百餘家，豪猾，二千石莫能制，於是景帝拜都為濟南守。

translations.literal: In Jinan the Jian clan kin numbered more than three hundred households—powerful and crafty; two-thousand-bushel officials could not control them; thereupon Emperor Jing appointed Du Jinan grand administrator.
translations.idiomatic: More than three hundred households of the powerful Jian lineage dominated Jinan beyond any governor's control, so Emperor Jing named Zhi Du to the post.

sentences.zh: 至則誅瞷氏首惡，餘皆股慄。

translations.literal: When he arrived he executed the Jian ringleaders; the rest all thigh trembled.
translations.idiomatic: On arrival he executed the clan's chiefs; everyone else shook with fear.

sentences.zh: 居歲餘，郡中不拾遺，旁十餘郡守畏都如大府。

translations.literal: After residing slightly more than a year, within the commandery none picked up lost articles; neighboring more than ten commanderies' guards feared Du like a great prefecture.
translations.idiomatic: Within a year lost goods lay untouched in the streets, and a dozen neighboring governors feared him like a senior minister.

sentences.zh: 都為人，勇有氣，公廉，不發私書，問遺無所受，請寄無所聽。

translations.literal: Du as a person was brave with spirit, fair and incorrupt—did not open private letters; inquiries and gifts he accepted none; requests and entrustments he heard none.
translations.idiomatic: Zhi Du was bold and upright: no private mail was opened, no gift or plea got a hearing.

sentences.zh: 常稱曰：「已背親而出身，固當奉職死節官下，終不顧妻子矣。」

translations.literal: He often said: "Having turned my back on kin and gone forth to serve, I ought to devote myself until death with integrity beneath office—I will never turn back to wife and children."
translations.idiomatic: He used to say, "I left family to serve the throne; I owe it my life at post and will not fuss over wife and children."

sentences.zh: 都遷為中尉，丞相條侯至貴居也，而都揖丞相。

translations.literal: Du was promoted to commandant of the capital; Chancellor Marquis Tiao held supreme rank in residence, yet Du bowed with hands clasped only at waist level to the chancellor.
translations.idiomatic: Promoted to metropolitan commandant, he greeted even the mighty Chancellor Zhou Yafu with no more than a waist-level bow.

sentences.zh: 是時，民樸，畏罪自重，而都獨先嚴酷，致行法不避貴戚，列侯宗室見都側目而視，號曰「蒼鷹」。

translations.literal: At this time the people were simple, feared crime and weighted themselves; yet Du alone led with stern cruelty, enforced law without avoiding noble affines—ranked marquises and imperial kin viewed Du sidelong and called him "Grey Eagle."
translations.idiomatic: The populace was still law-abiding, yet Zhi Du pioneered pitiless justice, sparing neither in-laws nor titled kin; they glared sidelong and nicknamed him the Blue Hawk.

sentences.zh: 臨江王征詣中尉府對簿，臨江王欲得刀筆為書謝上，而都禁吏弗與。

translations.literal: The Prince of Linjiang was summoned to the commandant's yamen to answer charges; the Prince of Linjiang wished to obtain knife and brush to write thanking the sovereign, but Du forbade clerks and did not give.
translations.idiomatic: Prince Rong of Linjiang was hauled to headquarters for interrogation and begged for knife and stylus to draft an apology to the throne; Zhi Du barred clerks from supplying them.

sentences.zh: 魏其侯使人間予臨江王。

translations.literal: Marquis Weiqi sent someone secretly to give the Prince of Linjiang.
translations.idiomatic: The Marquis of Weiqi smuggled writing tools to the prince.

sentences.zh: 臨江王既得，為書謝上，因自殺。

translations.literal: Once the Prince of Linjiang obtained them, he wrote thanking the sovereign and thereupon killed himself.
translations.idiomatic: With them he penned his apology and took his own life.

sentences.zh: 竇太后聞之，怒，以危法中都，都免歸家。

translations.literal: Empress Dowager Dou heard it, grew angry, struck Du with perilous law; Du was dismissed and returned home.
translations.idiomatic: Empress Dowager Dou, enraged, pinned a capital offense on Zhi Du and dismissed him.

sentences.zh: 景帝乃使使即拜都為雁門太守，便道之官，得以便宜從事。

translations.literal: Emperor Jing then dispatched envoys on the spot to appoint Du Yanmen grand administrator; by convenient route went to office—obtained all matters convenient advantage.
translations.idiomatic: Emperor Jing immediately commissioned him Yanmen governor by express edict with discretionary powers en route.

sentences.zh: 匈奴素聞郅都節，舉邊為引兵去，竟都死不近雁門。

translations.literal: The Xiongnu always heard of Zhi Du's integrity—withdrew troops along the whole border and departed; to the end of Du's death they did not approach Yanmen.
translations.idiomatic: The Xiongnu, respecting his iron integrity, pulled back from the frontier and never again pressed Yanmen while he lived.

sentences.zh: 匈奴至為偶人像都，令騎馳射，莫能中，其見憚如此。

translations.literal: The Xiongnu even made dummy images of Du, ordered riders to gallop and shoot—none could hit; his being feared was thus.
translations.idiomatic: They fashioned straw effigies of him for mounted archery drills that no one could strike—such was their dread.

sentences.zh: 匈奴患之。

translations.literal: The Xiongnu worried over him.
translations.idiomatic: They regarded him as a scourge.

sentences.zh: 乃中都以漢法。

translations.literal: Thereupon struck Du with Han law.
translations.idiomatic: They maneuvered the Han court into indicting him.

sentences.zh: 景帝曰：「都忠臣。

translations.literal: Emperor Jing said: "Du is a loyal minister."
translations.idiomatic: Emperor Jing protested, "Zhi Du is a loyal servant."

sentences.zh: 」欲釋之。

translations.literal: "He wished to release him.
translations.idiomatic: He meant to spare him.

sentences.zh: 竇太后曰：「臨江王獨非忠臣乎？

translations.literal: Empress Dowager Dou said: "Was the Prince of Linjiang alone not a loyal minister?"
translations.idiomatic: The dowager retorted, "Was Prince Rong not loyal?"

sentences.zh: 」於是斬都也。

translations.literal: "Thereupon Du was beheaded.
translations.idiomatic: Zhi Du went to the executioner's block.

sentences.zh: 甯成，南陽穰人也。

translations.literal: Ning Cheng was a man of Rang in Nanyang.
translations.idiomatic: Ning Cheng came from Rang in Nanyang Commandery.

sentences.zh: 以郎謁者事景帝。

translations.literal: As gentleman usher he served Emperor Jing.
translations.idiomatic: He served Emperor Jing as a gentleman usher.

sentences.zh: 好氣，為小吏，必陵其長吏；

translations.literal: He loved dominance; as petty clerk he invariably lorded over his senior clerks;
translations.idiomatic: Combative by nature, he browbeat superiors even as a junior clerk;

sentences.zh: 為人上，操下急如束濕。

translations.literal: when above others, he handled subordinates tight as binding wet hemp.
translations.idiomatic: once in charge, he squeezed subordinates as though wringing soaked rope.

sentences.zh: 猾賊任威。

translations.literal: Crafty villain employed intimidation.
translations.idiomatic: Cunning and ruthless, he ruled by terror.

sentences.zh: 稍遷至濟南都尉，而郅都為守。

translations.literal: Gradually promoted to Jinan commandant while Zhi Du was grand administrator.
translations.idiomatic: He rose to commandant of Jinan while Zhi Du governed there.

sentences.zh: 始前數都尉步入府，因吏謁守如縣令，其畏都如此。

translations.literal: Formerly those several commandants always entered the yamen on foot, through clerks visited the grand administrator like county chiefs—such did they fear Du.
translations.idiomatic: Earlier commandants had walked to headquarters and petitioned the governor through petty officers like county magistrates—such was Zhi Du's grip.

sentences.zh: 及成往，直凌都出其上。

translations.literal: When Cheng arrived, straightaway rode roughshod over Du and rose above him.
translations.idiomatic: Ning Cheng strode in and topped even Zhi Du.

sentences.zh: 都素聞其聲，善遇，與結歡。

translations.literal: Du always heard his reputation, treated him well, and bonded in friendship.
translations.idiomatic: Zhi Du, knowing his name, treated him generously and won his goodwill.

sentences.zh: 久之，都死，後長安左右宗室多犯法，上召成為中尉。

translations.literal: After long time Du died; afterward Chang'an left-right imperial kin many violated law; the sovereign summoned Cheng as commandant of the capital.
translations.idiomatic: After Zhi Du died, imperial relatives around Chang'an ran riot until the emperor recalled Ning Cheng as metropolitan commandant.

sentences.zh: 其治效郅都，其廉弗如，然宗室豪傑人皆惴恐。

translations.literal: His governance matched Zhi Du's effects; his incorruptibility did not compare—yet imperial clan heroes all trembled in terror.
translations.idiomatic: His methods rivaled Zhi Du's ferocity without the same honesty; still every noble house quaked.

sentences.zh: 武帝即位，徙為內史。

translations.literal: When Emperor Wu ascended, he was transferred privy treasurer.
translations.idiomatic: Emperor Wu moved him to privy treasurer of the capital precincts.

sentences.zh: 外戚多毀成之短，抵罪髡鉗。

translations.literal: Outer affines largely slandered Cheng's shortcomings; he met crime—shaved and collared.
translations.idiomatic: In-laws trumped up charges until he was shorn and fitted with an iron collar.

sentences.zh: 是時，九卿死即死，少被刑，而成刑極，自以為不復收，及解脫，詐刻傳出關歸家。

translations.literal: At this time among Nine Ministers death meant death—few suffered mutilation—yet Cheng's punishments reached the extreme; he thought himself never to be taken again; when freed, he forged a passport and slipped past the passes homeward.
translations.idiomatic: High ministers usually faced execution, not mutilation; Ning Cheng's sentence was cruel enough that he assumed his career over—until he forged papers, fled the frontier, and bolted home.

sentences.zh: 稱曰：「仕不至二千石，賈不至千萬，安可比人乎！

translations.literal: He declared: "If office does not reach two thousand bushels, if trade does not reach ten million—how can one compare with others!"
translations.idiomatic: He announced, "Without a two-thousand-bushel post or a ten-million fortune you cannot measure yourself against other men!"

sentences.zh: 」乃貰貣陂田千餘頃，假貧民，役使數千家。

translations.literal: "Thereupon he borrowed against more than a thousand qing of marsh acreage, lent to poor people, corvée-employed several thousand households.
translations.idiomatic: He borrowed capital to bankroll over a thousand qing of diked marsh, leased plots to the destitute, and drafted thousands of households as labor.

sentences.zh: 數年，會赦，致產數千萬，為任俠，持吏長短，出從數十騎。

translations.literal: After several years, met amnesty; accumulated property to several tens of millions; acted knight-errant; held clerks' secrets; went out followed by several tens of mounted riders.
translations.idiomatic: Amnesty restored him; within years his fortune ran to tens of millions. Playing vigilante, he blackmailed officials and rode abroad with dozens of horsemen.

sentences.zh: 其使民，威重於郡守。

translations.literal: His commanding common people—awe weighed heavier than grand administrators.
translations.idiomatic: Commoners feared his word more than their governor's.

sentences.zh: 周陽由

translations.literal: Zhouyang You.
translations.idiomatic: Zhouyang You.

sentences.zh: 周陽由，其父趙兼以淮南王舅侯周陽，故因氏焉。

translations.literal: Zhouyang You—his father Zhao Jian, because he was maternal uncle to the Prince of Huainan, was enfeoffed at Zhouyang—therefore took that surname.
translations.idiomatic: Zhouyang You inherited the surname because his father Zhao Jian, uncle to the king of Huainan, had been marquis of Zhouyang.

sentences.zh: 由以宗家任為郎，事文帝。

translations.literal: You by imperial-clan appointment became gentleman and served Emperor Wen.
translations.idiomatic: Through clan privilege he entered the gentleman corps under Emperor Wen.

sentences.zh: 景帝時，由為郡守。

translations.literal: In Emperor Jing's time You was grand administrator.
translations.idiomatic: Under Emperor Jing he served as a governor.

sentences.zh: 武帝即位，吏治尚修謹，然由居二千石中最為暴酷驕恣。

translations.literal: When Emperor Wu ascended, clerk governance still esteemed cultivation and caution—yet among two-thousand-bushel officials You was the most violent, cruel, arrogant, and wilful.
translations.idiomatic: Emperor Wu's early bureaucracy prized sobriety, yet Zhouyang You outdid every governor in brutality and swagger.

sentences.zh: 所愛者，撓法活之；

translations.literal: Those he loved—he bent law to keep them alive;
translations.idiomatic: Favorites walked free while statutes twisted around them;

sentences.zh: 所憎者，曲法滅之。

translations.literal: those he hated—he twisted law to destroy them.
translations.idiomatic: foes died with law bent to crush them.

sentences.zh: 所居郡，必夷其豪。

translations.literal: In whatever commandery he dwelt, he invariably extirpated its powerful families.
translations.idiomatic: Wherever he served he slaughtered local magnates.

sentences.zh: 為守，視都尉如令；

translations.literal: As grand administrator he treated commandants like county magistrates;
translations.idiomatic: As governor he treated his commandant like a county chief;

sentences.zh: 為都尉，陵太守，奪之治。

translations.literal: as commandant he rode roughshod over grand administrators and seized their governance.
translations.idiomatic: as commandant he bullied governors and stole their authority.

sentences.zh: 汲黯為忮，司馬安之文惡，俱在二千石列，同車未嘗敢均茵馮。

translations.literal: Ji An was harsh; Sima An's literary severity was wicked—both stood in the two-thousand-bushel ranks; riding together they never dared equal cushion mats.
translations.idiomatic: Ji An's vinegar temper and Sima An's poison pen shared two-thousand-bushel rank, yet neither would share a carriage seat evenly with Zhouyang You.

sentences.zh: 後由為河東都尉，與其守勝屠公爭權，相告言，勝屠公當抵罪，義不受刑，自殺，而由棄市。

translations.literal: Later You became Hedong commandant, wrangled power with his governor Shengtu Gong, mutually informed on each other; Shengtu Gong ought to meet crime, on principle refused to suffer punishment, killed himself; while You was exposed in market.
translations.idiomatic: Later, as Hedong commandant, he feuded with Governor Shengtu Gong until both denounced the other; Shengtu Gong chose suicide over sentence and Zhouyang You died by public execution.

sentences.zh: 自甯成、周陽由之後，事益多，民巧法，大抵吏治類多成、由等矣。

translations.literal: After Ning Cheng and Zhouyang You, affairs steadily multiplied; the people grew crafty with law; broadly clerk governance mostly resembled Cheng, You, and the like.
translations.idiomatic: After Ning Cheng and Zhouyang You, lawsuits multiplied, subjects learned legal dodges, and officials nationwide mimicked their ruthlessness.

sentences.zh: 趙禹，斄人也。

translations.literal: Zhao Yu was a man of Tai.
translations.idiomatic: Zhao Yu hailed from Tai (in Fufeng).

sentences.zh: 以佐史補中都官，用廉為令史，事太尉周亞夫。

translations.literal: By assistant clerk supplemented capital offices; by incorrupt became chief clerk, served Grand Commandant Zhou Yafu.
translations.idiomatic: He rose from aide to capital posts, won recommendation as honest, became chief clerk under Grand Commandant Zhou Yafu.

sentences.zh: 亞夫為丞相，禹為丞相史，府中皆稱其廉平。

translations.literal: When Yafu was chancellor, Yu was chancellor clerk; within the bureau all praised his incorrupt fairness.
translations.idiomatic: When Zhou Yafu became chancellor, Zhao Yu served as his bureau clerk and won praise for integrity.

sentences.zh: 然亞夫弗任，曰：「極知禹無害，然文深，不可以居大府。

translations.literal: Yet Yafu did not employ him, saying: "I utterly know Yu is harmless—yet his documents bite deep; cannot dwell in the great bureau."
translations.idiomatic: Yet Yafu hesitated: "I know Zhao Yu is upright, but his legal prose is merciless—not fit for the main office."

sentences.zh: 」武帝時，禹以刀筆吏積勞，遷為御史。

translations.literal: "In Emperor Wu's time Yu by knife-brush clerk accumulated merit, promoted imperial clerk.
translations.idiomatic: Under Emperor Wu he climbed from scribal clerk to imperial clerk.

sentences.zh: 上以為能，至中大夫。

translations.literal: The sovereign deemed him capable—to palace grandee.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor judged him capable and promoted him to palace grandee.

sentences.zh: 與張湯論定律令，作見知，吏傳相監司以法，盡自此始。

translations.literal: Together with Zhang Tang deliberated fixing statutes and commands, enacted "see and know"; clerks transmitting mutual surveillance by law—all began from this.
translations.idiomatic: With Zhang Tang he framed statutes, invented mutual-liability prosecutions, and chained clerks into watchdogging one another—precedents starting here.

sentences.zh: 禹為人廉裾，為吏以來，捨無食客。

translations.literal: Yu as a person was incorrupt and aloof; since becoming clerk, dwelling had no retained guests.
translations.idiomatic: Aloof and clean, he kept no hangers-on after entering office.

sentences.zh: 公卿相造請，禹終不行報謝，務在絕知友賓客之請，孤立行一意而已。

translations.literal: Dukes and ministers mutually visited requesting—Yu to the end did not go repay thanks; task lay in cutting off knowledge-friends and guests' requests—stood alone executing single intent.
translations.idiomatic: High ministers' courtesy calls went unanswered; he shut out friends and patrons to pursue one relentless course.

sentences.zh: 見法輒取，亦不復案求官屬陰罪。

translations.literal: Seeing law then took it—also did not again investigate seeking subordinates' hidden crimes.
translations.idiomatic: Where statute pointed, he struck—without fishing for secret faults among staff.

sentences.zh: 嘗中廢，已為廷尉。

translations.literal: Once middle-dismissed; afterward became commandant of justice.
translations.idiomatic: Briefly cashiered, he returned as commandant of justice.

sentences.zh: 始條侯以禹賊深，及禹為少府九卿，酷急。

translations.literal: Originally Marquis Tiao thought Yu crafty-deep; when Yu became Privy Treasurer among Nine Ministers—cruel and pressing.
translations.idiomatic: Marquis Zhou Yafu had called him dangerously subtle; as privy treasurer among the nine ministers he proved fierce and relentless.

sentences.zh: 至晚節，事益多。

translations.literal: Reaching late years, affairs steadily increased.
translations.idiomatic: Late in life, as caseloads swelled,

sentences.zh: 吏務為嚴峻，而禹治加緩，名為平。

translations.literal: clerks strove to be stern and severe—yet Yu's governance added slackness; his name was "even."
translations.idiomatic: underlings chased ever harsher methods while Zhao Yu deliberately eased his touch and earned a reputation for balance.

sentences.zh: 王溫舒等後起，治峻禹。

translations.literal: Wang Wenxu and others rose after—governance steep beyond Yu.
translations.idiomatic: Men like Wang Wenxu who followed outdid him in severity.

sentences.zh: 禹以老，徙為燕相，數歲，悖亂有罪，免歸。

translations.literal: Yu because old was transferred to be Yan kingdom chancellor—several years, rebellious disorder brought crime; dismissed, returned home.
translations.idiomatic: Advanced age won him the chancellorship of Yan; within a few years he was implicated in disorder and sent home in disgrace.

sentences.zh: 後十餘年，以壽卒於家。

translations.literal: After more than ten years, by natural span died at home.
translations.idiomatic: More than a decade later he died peacefully at home.

sentences.zh: 義縱，河東人也。

translations.literal: Yi Zong was a man of Hedong.
translations.idiomatic: Yi Zong came from Hedong.

sentences.zh: 少年時嘗與張次公俱攻剽，為群盜。

translations.literal: In youth he once together with Zhang Cigong both attacked and raided, acting bandit gang.
translations.idiomatic: As a youth he and Zhang Cigong had robbed together as outlaws.

sentences.zh: 縱有姊，以醫幸王太后。

translations.literal: Zong had an elder sister who by medical skill won favor with Empress Dowager Wang.
translations.idiomatic: Yi Zong's sister was a physician who caught the eye of Empress Dowager Wang.

sentences.zh: 太后問：「有子、兄弟為官者乎？

translations.literal: The empress dowager asked: "Do you have sons or brothers serving as officials?"
translations.idiomatic: She asked whether any sons or brothers held office.

sentences.zh: 」姊曰：「有弟無行，不可。

translations.literal: "The sister said: "I have a younger brother, but he lacks good conduct—not acceptable.""
translations.idiomatic: The woman answered, "I have a younger brother, but his conduct is poor—he would not do."

sentences.zh: 」太后乃告上，上拜義姁弟縱為中郎，補上黨郡中令。

translations.literal: "The empress dowager then informed the sovereign; the sovereign appointed Yi Xu's younger brother Zong as gentleman of the palace, supplemented Shangdang commandery interior chief.
translations.idiomatic: The dowager spoke to the emperor, who named her brother Yi Zong a gentleman cadet and posted him as interior chief of Shangdang.

sentences.zh: 治敢往，少溫籍，縣無逋事，舉第一。

translations.literal: In governance he dared press forward, showed little mild courtesy; the county had no backlog—raised first in rank.
translations.idiomatic: He governed boldly if bluntly, cleared every backlog, and topped the merit list.

sentences.zh: 遷為長陵及長安令，直法行治，不避貴戚。

translations.literal: Promoted chief of Changling and chief of Chang'an—straight law and enforcement, did not avoid noble affines.
translations.idiomatic: Promoted to magistrate of Changling and then Chang'an, he enforced the code without fear of powerful connections.

sentences.zh: 以捕按太后外孫脩成子中，上以為能，遷為河內都尉。

translations.literal: By arresting and investigating the empress dowager's maternal grandson Xiucheng's son Zhong—the sovereign deemed him capable and promoted him Henan commandant.
translations.idiomatic: His roundup of the empress dowager's grandson Xiucheng Zhong impressed the throne, and he became military governor of Henan.

sentences.zh: 至則族滅其豪穰氏之屬，河內道不拾遺。

translations.literal: On arrival he exterminated the powerful Rang clan and their kind—within Hedong none picked up lost articles on the road.
translations.idiomatic: He wiped out the predatory Rang lineage; afterward lost goods lay untouched in Hedong.

sentences.zh: 而張次公亦為郎，以勇悍從軍，敢深入，有功，封為岸頭侯。

translations.literal: Zhang Cigong also became a gentleman; by fierce bravery he followed the army, dared deep penetration, earned merit, and was enfeoffed Marquis of Antou.
translations.idiomatic: Zhang Cigong too entered the gentleman corps, fought with conspicuous daring, and was made marquis of Antou.

sentences.zh: 甯成家居，上欲以為郡守，御史大夫弘曰：「臣居山東為小吏時，甯成為濟南都尉，其治如狼牧羊，成不可令治民。

translations.literal: Ning Cheng lived at home; the sovereign wished to make him grand administrator—Imperial Clerk Hong said: "When I was a petty clerk east of the mountains, Cheng was Jinan commandant; his rule was like a wolf herding sheep—Cheng must not be set over common people."
translations.idiomatic: The emperor considered Ning Cheng for a governorship, but Gongsun Hong objected: "As Jinan commandant he shepherded men like a wolf among sheep; do not put him over civilians."

sentences.zh: 」上乃拜成為關都尉。

translations.literal: "The sovereign then appointed Cheng pass commandant.
translations.idiomatic: Ning Cheng was named commandant of the imperial passes instead.

sentences.zh: 歲餘，關吏稅肄郡國出入關者，號曰：「寧見乳虎，無直甯成之怒。

translations.literal: "After slightly more than a year, pass clerks levied fees on travelers entering and leaving from the commanderies—a proverb ran: "Better meet a suckling tigress than cross Ning Cheng's temper.""
translations.idiomatic: Within a year frontier officers taxing travelers coined the saying: "Face a nursing tigress before you brave Ning Cheng's rage."

sentences.zh: 」其暴如此。

translations.literal: "Such was his brutality.
translations.idiomatic: Such was his savagery.

sentences.zh: 義縱自河內遷為南陽太守，聞甯成家居南陽，及至關，甯成側行送迎，然縱氣盛，弗為禮。

translations.literal: Yi Zong transferred from Hedong to Nanyang grand administrator; hearing Ning Cheng lived retired in Nanyang, when Zong reached the pass Cheng walked sideways to greet him—yet Zong's spirit swelled and he returned no courtesy.
translations.idiomatic: Yi Zong learned Ning Cheng had retired to Nanyang and met him at the frontier; Cheng cringed aside in welcome, but Yi Zong brushed past without nodding.

sentences.zh: 至郡，遂按甯氏，破碎其家。

translations.literal: Reaching the commandery, he then investigated the Ning clan and shattered their household.
translations.idiomatic: In office he dismantled the Ning family fortune.

sentences.zh: 成坐有罪，及孔、暴之屬皆奔亡，南陽吏民重足一跡。

translations.literal: Cheng sat with crime; Kong, Bao, and their like all fled; Nanyang clerks and people stood as if sharing one footprint.
translations.idiomatic: Ning Cheng fell under indictment while other magnates fled; officials and townsfolk were too frightened to shift their feet.

sentences.zh: 而平氏硃強、杜衍杜周為縱爪牙之吏，任用，遷為廷尉史。

translations.literal: Meanwhile Zhu Qiang of Pingshi and Du Zhou of Duyan served as Zong's fang-and-claw clerks—employed—and were promoted commandants of justice clerks.
translations.idiomatic: Zhu Qiang and Du Zhou became his enforcers and rose to clerks of the commandant of justice.

sentences.zh: 軍數出定襄，定襄吏民亂敗，於是徙縱為定襄太守。

translations.literal: The army repeatedly campaigned from Dingxiang; Dingxiang's clerks and people were thrown into chaos—thereupon Zong was moved to Dingxiang grand administrator.
translations.idiomatic: Repeated campaigns from Dingxiang had wrecked local order, so Yi Zong was sent there as governor.

sentences.zh: 縱至，掩定襄獄中重罪二百餘人，及賓客昆弟私入相視者亦二百餘人。

translations.literal: When Zong arrived he seized more than two hundred heavy offenders in Dingxiang jail—and guests and brothers who had slipped in for private visits numbered another two hundred.
translations.idiomatic: He swept up over two hundred capital defendants plus another two hundred visitors—kin and clients who had stolen into the jail.

sentences.zh: 縱一切捕鞠，曰「為死罪解脫」。

translations.literal: Zong arrested and interrogated them wholesale, declaring they "aided capital felons to escape."
translations.idiomatic: He charged the lot with abetting condemned prisoners.

sentences.zh: 是日皆報殺四百餘人。

translations.literal: That same day more than four hundred were executed on report.
translations.idiomatic: That day the execution rolls carried over four hundred names.

sentences.zh: 郡中不寒而慄，猾民佐吏為治。

translations.literal: Within the commandery people shivered without cold; crafty folk assisted clerks in governing.
translations.idiomatic: The district shook with dread; even sharp operators hurried to collaborate with the yamen.

sentences.zh: 是時，趙禹、張湯為九卿矣，然其治尚寬，輔法而行，縱以鷹擊毛摯為治。

translations.literal: At this time Zhao Yu and Zhang Tang already ranked among the Nine Ministers—yet their style stayed comparatively lenient, following statute—while Zong governed by hawk strikes and tearing feathers.
translations.idiomatic: Zhao Yu and Zhang Tang were harsh ministers already, but next to Yi Zong's eagle tactics they looked almost moderate.

sentences.zh: 後會更五銖錢白金起，民為奸，京師尤甚，乃以縱為右內史，王溫舒為中尉。

translations.literal: Later, when the new wuzhu coinage and white-metal currency spawned fraud—worst in the capital—Zong was made right privy treasurer and Wang Wenxu metropolitan commandant.
translations.idiomatic: When debased coinage drove capital fraud, the court named Yi Zong right metropolitan superintendent and Wang Wenxu commandant of the guard.

sentences.zh: 溫舒至惡，所為弗先言縱，縱必以氣陵之，敗壞其功。

translations.literal: Wenxu was utterly vicious; whatever he undertook he did not clear with Zong first—Zong invariably rode roughshod over him in spirit and ruined his schemes.
translations.idiomatic: Wang Wenxu was vicious and freelanced his raids until Yi Zong sabotaged him out of sheer rivalry.

sentences.zh: 其治，所誅殺甚多，然取為小治，奸益不勝，直指始出矣。

translations.literal: Their governance slew vast numbers—yet counted only as petty policing—wickedness grew unstoppable—and imperial "direct investigators" began rolling out.
translations.idiomatic: The slaughter looked like small-scale policing even as crime exploded and palace inspectors fanned into the provinces.

sentences.zh: 吏之治以斬殺縛吏為務，閻奉以惡用矣。

translations.literal: Clerk governance made beheading and binding its chief task—Yan Feng earned employment through viciousness.
translations.idiomatic: Officialdom raced to kill and bind; Yan Feng won promotion through sheer cruelty.

sentences.zh: 縱廉，其治效郅都。

translations.literal: Zong was incorrupt; his administrative effects matched Zhi Du's.
translations.idiomatic: Yi Zong stayed honest and governed with Zhi Du's pitiless edge.

sentences.zh: 上幸鼎湖，病久，已而卒起幸甘泉，道不治。

translations.literal: The sovereign favored Dinghu—ill a long while—then suddenly rose to favor Sweet Springs—the road went unrepaired.
translations.idiomatic: After a long illness at Dinghu Lake the emperor abruptly traveled to Sweet Springs while the highway stayed broken.

sentences.zh: 上怒曰：「縱以我為不行此道乎？

translations.literal: The sovereign angrily said: "Does Zong imagine I will never take this road?"
translations.idiomatic: He snapped, "Does Yi Zong think I intend to abandon this route?"

sentences.zh: 」銜之。

translations.literal: "He harbored resentment.
translations.idiomatic: Yi Zong nursed a grudge.

sentences.zh: 至冬，楊可方受告緡，縱以為此亂民，部吏捕其為可使者。

translations.literal: Come winter Yang Ke was about to enforce the denunciation of concealed wealth—Zong deemed this would riot the people and ordered clerks to arrest Yang Ke's agents.
translations.idiomatic: When Yang Ke launched the wealth-reporting inquisitions, Yi Zong called them destabilizing and seized Yang Ke's deputies.

sentences.zh: 天子聞，使杜式治，以為廢格沮事，棄縱市。

translations.literal: The Son of Heaven heard, sent Du Shi to judge him—found him guilty of obstructing imperial directives—and had Yi Zong executed in the marketplace.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor charged Du Shi with the case, convicted Yi Zong of blocking state policy, and left him on the execution ground.

sentences.zh: 後一歲，張湯亦死。

translations.literal: One year later Zhang Tang also died.
translations.idiomatic: Zhang Tang died the following year.

sentences.zh: 王溫舒

translations.literal: Wang Wenxu.
translations.idiomatic: Wang Wenxu.

sentences.zh: 王溫舒，陽陵人也。

translations.literal: Wang Wenxu was a man of Yangling.
translations.idiomatic: Wang Wenxu came from Yangling.

sentences.zh: 少時椎埋為奸。

translations.literal: In youth he buried victims for profit and broke the law.
translations.idiomatic: As a youth he ran burial rackets and worse.

sentences.zh: 已而試縣亭長，數廢。

translations.literal: Later he tried office as county pavilion chief—several times dismissed.
translations.idiomatic: Trial postings as a pavilion chief ended in repeated dismissal.

sentences.zh: 數為吏，以治獄至廷尉史。

translations.literal: Repeatedly a clerk—by judging prisons rose to commandant of justice clerk.
translations.idiomatic: He climbed through jail work to clerkships under the commandant of justice.

sentences.zh: 事張湯，遷為御史，督盜賊，殺傷甚多。

translations.literal: He served Zhang Tang—promoted imperial clerk—supervised robbers—killings and wounds were many.
translations.idiomatic: Under Zhang Tang he became an imperial clerk hunting bandits with bloody zeal.

sentences.zh: 稍遷至廣平都尉，擇郡中豪敢往吏十餘人為爪牙，皆把其陰重罪，而縱使督盜賊，快其意所欲得。

translations.literal: Gradually promoted Guangping commandant—selected more than ten bold clerks of the commandery as claws—each held their hidden capital crimes—then unleashed them to hunt robbers and seize whatever pleased him.
translations.idiomatic: As Guangping commandant he blackmailed a dozen fearless clerks with capital charges, then turned them loose as bandit-hunters.

sentences.zh: 此人雖有百罪，弗法；

translations.literal: Though such a man might commit a hundred offenses—the law did not touch him;
translations.idiomatic: Those henchmen could rack up countless crimes untouched;

sentences.zh: 即有避回，夷之，亦滅宗。

translations.literal: if they shrank back—exterminate them—and wipe out their lineage.
translations.idiomatic: hesitate once and he extirpated them root and branch.

sentences.zh: 以故齊趙之郊盜不敢近廣平，廣平聲為道不拾遺。

translations.literal: Therefore along the Qi-Zhao marches robbers dared not approach Guangping—Guangping's fame became "none picked up lost goods on the road."
translations.idiomatic: Bandits avoided his borders until Guangping passed for the kingdom where no one pocketed lost property.

sentences.zh: 上聞，遷為河內太守。

translations.literal: The sovereign heard and promoted him Hedong grand administrator.
translations.idiomatic: Word reached the throne and he became governor of Hedong.

sentences.zh: 素居廣平時，皆知河內豪奸之家。

translations.literal: While stationed at Guangping he had mapped every powerful rogue household in Hedong.
translations.idiomatic: Even in Guangping he had catalogued Hedong's criminal magnates.

sentences.zh: 及往，以九月至，令郡具私馬五十匹，為驛自河內至長安，部吏如居廣平時方略，捕郡中豪猾，相連坐千餘家。

translations.literal: When he went—arriving in the ninth month—he ordered the commandery to ready fifty private horses as a courier relay from Hedong to Chang'an; departmental clerks used his Guangping playbook, arresting powerful villains until more than a thousand households faced collective guilt.
translations.idiomatic: He arrived in autumn with fifty mounts couriering pleas to the capital, then rolled up over a thousand households on conspiracy charges.

sentences.zh: 上書請，大者至族，小者乃死，家盡沒入償臧。

translations.literal: He memorialized requesting—the great cases to clan extinction, lesser to death—households all confiscated to repay stolen goods.
translations.idiomatic: His memorial demanded clan executions for leaders, death for lesser offenders, and confiscations to cover stolen wealth.

sentences.zh: 奏行不過二日，得可，事論報，至流血十餘里。

translations.literal: The memorial cleared within two days—approval returned—sentences carried out until blood flowed for ten li along the road.
translations.idiomatic: Two days brought imperial assent; killings reddened the roads for miles.

sentences.zh: 河內皆怪其奏，以為神速。

translations.literal: All Hedong marveled at his memorial—deemed it supernatural speed.
translations.idiomatic: The commandery stood amazed at how fast he moved.

sentences.zh: 盡十二月，郡中無犬吠之盜。

translations.literal: Through the twelfth month the commandery heard no dog bark at thieves.
translations.idiomatic: By year's end not even dogs stirred at burglars.

sentences.zh: 其頗不得，失之旁郡，追求，會春，溫舒頓足漢曰：「嗟乎，令冬月益展一月，足吾事矣！

translations.literal: Those partly uncaught slipped into neighboring commanderies—he chased them—spring arrived—Wenxu stamped his foot and sighed to Heaven: "Ah—if winter could stretch one month longer I would finish my work!"
translations.idiomatic: Some fugitives escaped into other jurisdictions; when spring halted executions he stamped the earth and cried that another month of winter would have sealed his roundup.

sentences.zh: 」其好殺行威不愛人如此。

translations.literal: "Such was his love of killing and displaying awe without pity for others.
translations.idiomatic: Such was his thirst for slaughter masked as awe.

sentences.zh: 上聞之，以為能，遷為中尉。

translations.literal: The sovereign heard, deemed him capable, and promoted metropolitan commandant.
translations.idiomatic: Impressed, the emperor made him commandant of the guard.

sentences.zh: 其治復放河內，徒請召猜禍吏與從事，河內則楊皆、麻戊，關中揚贛、成信等。

translations.literal: His rule copied Hedong—summoning clerks who thrived on suspicion and ruin—Hedong supplied Yang Jie and Ma Wu; Guanzhong Yang Gan and Cheng Xin among others.
translations.idiomatic: He imported Hedong's poisoners—Yang Jie and Ma Wu—and paired them with Guanzhong bullies such as Yang Gan and Cheng Xin.

sentences.zh: 義縱為內史，憚之，未敢恣治。

translations.literal: Yi Zong as privy treasurer feared him and dared not govern wilfully.
translations.idiomatic: Privy treasurer Yi Zong kept clear of him.

sentences.zh: 及縱死，張湯敗後，徙為廷尉。

translations.literal: After Zong died and Zhang Tang fell he shifted to commandant of justice.
translations.idiomatic: Once Yi Zong and Zhang Tang were gone he rose to commandant of justice.

sentences.zh: 而尹齊為中尉坐法抵罪，溫舒復為中尉。

translations.literal: When Yin Qi as metropolitan commandant met conviction under law, Wenxu returned as metropolitan commandant.
translations.idiomatic: Yin Qi's conviction cleared the way for Wang Wenxu's second stint as commandant.

sentences.zh: 為人少文，居於它官不辯，至於中尉則心開。

translations.literal: As a man he showed little literary polish—in other posts he seemed dull—but once metropolitan commandant his mind opened.
translations.idiomatic: Slow-tongued in lesser offices, he turned ingenious once handed the capital guard.

sentences.zh: 素習關中俗，知豪惡吏，豪惡吏盡復為用。

translations.literal: Long versed in Guanzhong ways—he knew every vicious clerk—and put them all back to work.
translations.idiomatic: He knew Guanzhong's corrupt clerks intimately and reemployed every one.

sentences.zh: 吏苛察淫惡少年，投缿購告言奸，置伯落長以收司奸。

translations.literal: Clerks harshly screened dissolute youths—posted denunciation urns offering rewards for informing—and set bailuo chiefs to sniff out crime.
translations.idiomatic: They shook down rowdies, offered bounties through sealed accusation boxes, and stationed street captains to spy.

sentences.zh: 溫舒多諂，善事有勢者；

translations.literal: Wenxu flattered constantly—clever at serving the powerful;
translations.idiomatic: Wang Wenxu curried favor with anyone influential;

sentences.zh: 即無勢，視之如奴。

translations.literal: if powerless—he treated them like slaves.
translations.idiomatic: the powerless he handled like bondservants.

sentences.zh: 有勢家，雖有奸如山，弗犯；

translations.literal: Where families had pull—even guilt piled high as mountains—he did not touch;
translations.idiomatic: Connected houses—even guilt mountain-high—went untouched;

sentences.zh: 無勢，雖貴戚，必侵辱。

translations.literal: without pull—even imperial affines—he necessarily crushed and humiliated.
translations.idiomatic: the friendless—even royal in-laws—he trampled.

sentences.zh: 舞文巧，請下戶之猾，以動大豪。

translations.literal: He twisted documents craftily—netted petty sharpers—to frighten great magnates.
translations.idiomatic: He warped indictments to hook small fry and panic grandees.

sentences.zh: 其治中尉如此。

translations.literal: Such was his metropolitan command.
translations.idiomatic: Such was his tenure as commandant.

sentences.zh: 奸猾窮治，大氐盡靡爛獄中，行論無出者。

translations.literal: The crafty were hunted to ruin—most rotted in jail—sentenced without leaving alive.
translations.idiomatic: Suspects rarely left prison except as corpses.

sentences.zh: 其爪牙吏虎而冠。

translations.literal: His claw clerks were tigers in caps.
translations.idiomatic: His enforcers were tigers wearing official hats.

sentences.zh: 於是中尉部中中猾以下皆伏，有勢者為游聲譽，稱治。

translations.literal: Then within the commandant's jurisdiction middling villains downward all submitted—those with influence bought glowing reputations—praised as well governed.
translations.idiomatic: Petty crooks vanished while the connected purchased praise for "good order."

sentences.zh: 數歲，其吏多以權貴富。

translations.literal: Within several years many of his clerks grew rich on connections.
translations.idiomatic: Within years his staff grew wealthy peddling influence.

sentences.zh: 溫舒擊東越還，議有不中意，坐以法免。

translations.literal: Wenxu returned from striking Eastern Yue—his counsel displeased—and was dismissed under law.
translations.idiomatic: His Eastern Yue briefing annoyed the throne and cost him his post.

sentences.zh: 是時，上方欲作通天台而未有人，溫舒請復中尉脫卒，得數萬人作。

translations.literal: At this time the sovereign wished to build the Tongtian Terrace but lacked labor—Wenxu requested restoring metropolitan commandant convict laborers—obtained tens of thousands for construction.
translations.idiomatic: He volunteered convict gangs from the guard command to raise tens of thousands of laborers for the Tongtai project.

sentences.zh: 上說，拜為少府。

translations.literal: The sovereign pleased—appointed privy treasurer.
translations.idiomatic: The delighted emperor named him privy treasurer.

sentences.zh: 徙右內史，治如其故，奸邪少禁。

translations.literal: Transferred right privy treasurer—governed as before—wickedness slightly curbed.
translations.idiomatic: As right metropolitan superintendent he replayed old tactics with modest success.

sentences.zh: 坐法失官，復為右輔，行中尉，如故操。

translations.literal: Stripped by statute—again right metropolitan aide—acting metropolitan commandant—with former habits.
translations.idiomatic: Disgraced yet reassigned as acting commandant, he resumed his methods.

sentences.zh: 歲餘，會宛軍發，詔征豪吏。

translations.literal: After slightly more than a year—when the Wan expedition mobilized—the edict summoned harsh clerks.
translations.idiomatic: When the Wan campaign began the court called for ruthless officers.

sentences.zh: 溫舒匿其吏華成，及人有變告溫舒受員騎錢，它奸利事，罪至族，自殺。

translations.literal: Wenxu hid his clerk Hua Cheng—men accused him of taking bribes from mounted clerks and other graft—crime reaching clan extent—he killed himself.
translations.idiomatic: He concealed clerk Hua Cheng until informers tied him to bribery and capital graft; fearing execution he committed suicide.

sentences.zh: 其時，兩弟及兩婚家亦各自坐它罪而族。

translations.literal: At that time two younger brothers and both in-law families likewise fell on other charges and faced clan execution.
translations.idiomatic: His brothers and both marital clans were extinguished on separate indictments.

sentences.zh: 光祿勳徐自為曰：「悲夫！

translations.literal: Superintendent of the palace gentlemen Xu Ziwei said: "How tragic!"
translations.idiomatic: Palace superintendent Xu Ziwei lamented,

sentences.zh: 夫古有三族，而王溫舒罪至同時而五族乎！

translations.literal: "Ancient punishments reached three kin groups—yet Wang Wenxu's guilt struck five kin circles at once!"
translations.idiomatic: "The ancients wiped out three affines; Wang Wenxu destroyed five branches in one blow!"

sentences.zh: 」溫舒死，家累千金。

translations.literal: "Wenxu died—his household still piled a thousand gold.
translations.idiomatic: Even dead he left a thousand pounds of gold.

sentences.zh: 尹齊，東郡茌平人也。

translations.literal: Yin Qi was a man of Chiping in Dong commandery.
translations.idiomatic: Yin Qi hailed from Chiping in Dong commandery.

sentences.zh: 以刀筆吏稍遷至御史。

translations.literal: From knife-brush clerk he gradually rose imperial clerk.
translations.idiomatic: He climbed from scribal clerk to imperial clerk.

sentences.zh: 事張湯，湯數稱以為廉。

translations.literal: He served Zhang Tang—Tang repeatedly praised him incorrupt.
translations.idiomatic: Zhang Tang repeatedly praised his honesty.

sentences.zh: 武帝使督盜賊，斬伐不避貴勢。

translations.literal: Emperor Wu sent him to supervise robbers—executions spared no noble power.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor tasked him with bandit suppression regardless of rank.

sentences.zh: 遷關都尉，聲甚於甯成。

translations.literal: Promoted pass commandant—reputation exceeded Ning Cheng.
translations.idiomatic: As pass commandant he terrified travelers worse than Ning Cheng.

sentences.zh: 上以為能，拜為中尉。

translations.literal: The sovereign deemed him capable—appointed metropolitan commandant.
translations.idiomatic: Capable in the emperor's eyes, he became metropolitan commandant.

sentences.zh: 吏民益凋敝，輕齊木強少文，豪惡吏伏匿而善吏不能為治，以故事多廢，抵罪。

translations.literal: Clerks and people grew exhausted—disdained Qi's wooden stubbornness and lack of polish—powerful wicked clerks hid while honest clerks could not govern—therefore affairs largely collapsed—met conviction.
translations.idiomatic: His stiff manner exhausted the capital; villains hid while honest clerks stalled until scandal broke.

sentences.zh: 後復為淮陽都尉。

translations.literal: Later again Huaiyang commandant.
translations.idiomatic: He resurfaced as Huaiyang commandant.

sentences.zh: 王溫舒敗後數年，病死，家直不滿五十金。

translations.literal: Several years after Wang Wenxu fell—died of illness—household worth not fifty pounds of gold.
translations.idiomatic: Years after Wang Wenxu's crash he died sick, leaving less than fifty catties of gold.

sentences.zh: 所誅滅淮陽甚多，及死，仇家欲燒其屍，妻亡去，歸葬。

translations.literal: Those he exterminated in Huaiyang were many—when he died enemy families wished to burn his corpse—his wife fled—returned him for burial.
translations.idiomatic: Huaiyang nursed many grudges; mourners meant to burn his body until his wife stole it away for burial.

sentences.zh: 楊僕，宜陽人也。

translations.literal: Yang Pu was a man of Yiyang.
translations.idiomatic: Yang Pu came from Yiyang.

sentences.zh: 以千夫為吏。

translations.literal: By thousand-headman rank he became clerk.
translations.idiomatic: The thousand-headman noble rank bought him a clerkship.

sentences.zh: 河南守舉為御史，使督盜賊關東，治放尹齊，以敢擊行。

translations.literal: Henan grand administrator raised him imperial clerk—sent to supervise robbers east of the passes—governance modeled Yin Qi—acted by daring strikes.
translations.idiomatic: Henan's governor recommended him to hunt bandits beyond the passes in Yin Qi's brutal style.

sentences.zh: 稍遷至主爵都尉，上以為能。

translations.literal: Gradually promoted chief of merit nobility commandant—the sovereign deemed him capable.
translations.idiomatic: He rose to chief of merit nobilities with imperial praise.

sentences.zh: 南越反，拜為樓船將軍，有功，封將梁侯。

translations.literal: Southern Yue rebelled—appointed tower-ship general—had merit—enfeoffed General Liang marquis.
translations.idiomatic: He commanded the tower-ship fleet against Southern Yue and won the marquisate of Jiangliang.

sentences.zh: 東越反，上欲復使將，為其伐前勞，以書敕責之曰：「將軍之功，獨有先破石門、尋狹，非有斬將騫旗之實也，烏足以驕人哉！

translations.literal: Eastern Yue rebelled—the sovereign wished to employ him again—because he boasted prior labor—with document rebuked saying: "General's merit—only first breaking Shimen and Xinxia—not having beheaded a general or seized banners—how enough to pride yourself before others!"
translations.idiomatic: Planning a second campaign, the emperor sent a blistering edict: breaking Shimen Gorge hardly equals capturing enemy banners—why swagger?"

sentences.zh: 前破番禺，捕降者以為虜，掘死人以為獲，是一過也。

translations.literal: Earlier at Panyu you counted surrendered men as captives and dug up corpses as kills—that is one fault.
translations.idiomatic: At Panyu you branded surrendering soldiers as prisoners and claimed exhumed bones as kills—that was error one.

sentences.zh: 建德、呂嘉逆罪不容於天下，將軍擁精兵不窮追，超然以東越為援，是二過也。

translations.literal: Jiande and Lü Jia were rebels intolerable to the realm—yet the general hugged crack troops without pursuing to the end—aloof relied on Eastern Yue as backup—that is the second fault.
translations.idiomatic: Jiande and Lü Jia deserved pursuit to extinction; instead you hoarded elite troops and leaned on Eastern Yue—that was error two.

sentences.zh: 士卒暴露連歲，為朝會不置酒，將軍不念其勤勞，而造佞巧，請乘傳行塞，因用歸家，懷銀黃，垂三組，誇鄉里，是三過也。

translations.literal: The troops stood exposed year after year; court gatherings served no wine—yet the general never minded their toil and spun flattery, begging post-horses to tour the frontier just to detour home, wearing silver-gold seals with three ribbons dangling—boasting to his neighbors—that is the third fault.
translations.idiomatic: Soldiers froze and starved for years while court dinners went dry; instead of honoring their sweat you connived a pleasure trip along the barrier, detoured home flashing triple cords of office, and bragged in your village—that was fault three.

sentences.zh: 失期內顧，以道惡為解，失尊尊之序，是四過也。

translations.literal: Missing the deadline while looking homeward—you blamed bad roads—violating the order of precedence between lord and lord—that is the fourth fault.
translations.idiomatic: You blamed muddy roads for missing your rendezvous and broke due precedence between sovereign and servant—that was fault four.

sentences.zh: 欲請蜀刀，問君賈幾何，對曰率數百，武庫日出兵而陽不知，挾偽干君，是五過也。

translations.literal: You wished to request Shu blades—asked their price—answered they average several hundred—yet the arsenal issues arms daily while you feigned ignorance—bearing deceit before your lord—that is the fifth fault.
translations.idiomatic: You asked the price of Shu steel while pretending the armory was a mystery though it issues weapons daily—that fifth lie insulted the throne.

sentences.zh: 受詔不至蘭池宮，明日又不對。

translations.literal: Receiving summons you did not reach Orchid Pool Palace—next day again failed audience.
translations.idiomatic: You ignored a summons to Orchid Pool and skipped audience the next morning.

sentences.zh: 假令將軍之吏問之不對，令之不從，其罪何如？

translations.literal: Suppose your general's clerks questioned you and got no answer—orders disobeyed—what would their guilt be?
translations.idiomatic: If your own officers ignored summons as you did, what punishment would they deserve?

sentences.zh: 推此心以在外，江海之間可得信乎！

translations.literal: Extend this attitude abroad—could the rivers and seas trust you!
translations.idiomatic: Take that attitude to the field and no ally between river and sea would trust you.

sentences.zh: 今東越深入，將軍能率眾以掩過不？

translations.literal: Now Eastern Yue lies deep within—can the general lead the host to redeem these faults?
translations.idiomatic: Eastern Yue waits—will you lead your men to wipe this slate clean?

sentences.zh: 」僕惶恐，對曰：「願盡死贖罪！

translations.literal: "Pu trembling replied: "Willing to exhaust death redeeming crime!""
translations.idiomatic: Yang Pu stammered, "I will spend my life paying for these crimes!"

sentences.zh: 」與王溫舒俱破東越。

translations.literal: "Together with Wang Wenxu they shattered Eastern Yue.
translations.idiomatic: He joined Wang Wenxu to crush Eastern Yue.

sentences.zh: 後復與左將軍荀彘俱擊朝鮮，為彘所縛，語在《朝鮮傳》。

translations.literal: Later again with Left General Xun Zhi attacked Korea—was bound by Zhi—the account stands in the Korea biography.
translations.idiomatic: Later he campaigned in Korea beside General Xun Zhi and ended shackled—see the biography of Korea.

sentences.zh: 還，免為庶人，病死。

translations.literal: Returned—stripped to commoner—died of illness.
translations.idiomatic: Stripped of rank, he died a commoner.

sentences.zh: 咸宣，楊人也。

translations.literal: Xian Xuan was a man of Yang.
translations.idiomatic: Xian Xuan came from Yangyi.

sentences.zh: 以佐史給事河東守。

translations.literal: As assistant clerk he served Hedong grand administrator.
translations.idiomatic: He began as an aide to Hedong's governor.

sentences.zh: 衛將軍青使買馬河東，見宣無害，言上，徵為廄丞。

translations.literal: General Wei Qing sent men to buy horses in Hedong—saw Xuan harmless—reported upward—summoned stable assistant.
translations.idiomatic: Wei Qing met him buying horses in Hedong, judged him capable, and had him named assistant stablemaster.

sentences.zh: 官事辦，稍遷至御史及中丞，使治主父偃及淮南反獄，所以微文深詆殺者甚眾，稱為敢決疑。

translations.literal: Official tasks handled—gradually promoted imperial clerk and palace clerk—sent to judge Zhufu Yan and Huainan rebellion cases—thereby subtle writings deep slanders killed many—praised as daring to settle doubts.
translations.idiomatic: He rose through the censorate, prosecuted Zhufu Yan and the Huainan plot with cruel ingenuity, and won fame for ruthless verdicts.

sentences.zh: 數廢數起，為御史及中丞者幾二十歲。

translations.literal: Several times dismissed several times raised—as imperial clerk and palace clerk nearly twenty years.
translations.idiomatic: Over twenty years he bounced between cashiering and recall as censor and vice-censor.

sentences.zh: 王溫舒為中尉，而宣為左內史。

translations.literal: Wang Wenxu was metropolitan commandant while Xuan was left privy treasurer.
translations.idiomatic: When Wang Wenxu commanded the guard, Xian Xuan governed as left metropolitan superintendent.

sentences.zh: 其治米鹽，事小大皆關其手，自部署縣名曹寶物，官吏令丞弗得擅搖，痛以重法繩之。

translations.literal: His rule tracked rice and salt—great and small affairs passed through his hands—personally deployed county name-halls and treasures—clerks and magistrates could not stir without leave—painfully bound them with heavy law.
translations.idiomatic: He micromanaged every trifle, seized county offices and stores, blocked magistrates from acting, and crushed them under harsh statutes.

sentences.zh: 居官數年，一切為小治辯，然獨宣以小至大，能自行之，難以為經。

translations.literal: Dwelling in office several years—everything petty governance—but only Xuan could escalate small to large and execute alone—hard to take as constant rule.
translations.idiomatic: Years of petty tyranny worked only because Xian Xuan personally drove every detail—a model no one else could sustain.

sentences.zh: 中廢為右扶風，坐怒其吏成信，信亡藏上林中，宣使縣令將吏卒，闌入上林中蠶室門攻亭格殺信，射中苑門，宣下吏，為大逆當族，自殺。

translations.literal: Middle-dismissed as right metropolitan shepherd—sat because angry at clerk Cheng Xin—Xin fled hiding in Shanglin—Xuan ordered the county chief to lead clerks and troops—burst through Shanglin silkworm-room gate attacking pavilion—beat killed Xin—shot hit park gate—Xuan handed to judges—great rebellion deserved clan execution—suicide.
translations.idiomatic: Demoted to governor of Youfufeng, he hunted fugitive clerk Cheng Xin into the imperial park, stormed a gate, killed him and punctured the lodge wall—capital treason—and killed himself.

sentences.zh: 而杜周任用。

translations.literal: And Du Zhou received employment.
translations.idiomatic: Du Zhou stepped into his place.

sentences.zh: 是時，郡守尉、諸侯相、二千石欲為治者，大抵盡效王溫舒等，而吏民益輕犯法，盜賊滋起。

translations.literal: At this time grand administrators, commandants, feudal chancellors, two-thousand-bushel officials wishing to govern mostly imitated Wang Wenxu and company—yet clerks and people grew bolder breaking law—robbers-thieves swelled.
translations.idiomatic: Governors who wanted results copied Wang Wenxu until subjects shrugged at crime and bandit armies multiplied.

sentences.zh: 南陽有梅免、百政，楚有段中、杜少，齊有徐勃，燕、趙之間有堅盧、范主之屬。

translations.literal: Nanyang had Mei Mian and Bai Zheng; Chu had Duan Zhong and Du Shao; Qi had Xu Bo; between Yan and Zhao were Jian Lu and Fan Zhu and their kind.
translations.idiomatic: Nanyang bred Mei Mian and Bai Zheng; Chu had Duan Zhong and Du Shao; Qi had Xu Bo; Yan-Zhao spawned Jian Lu and Fan Zhu.

sentences.zh: 大群至數千人，擅自號，攻城邑，取庫兵，釋死罪，縛辱郡守、都尉，殺二千石，為檄告縣趨具食；

translations.literal: Great bands reached several thousand—self-proclaimed titles—attacked cities—seized arsenal arms—released capital convicts—bound and humiliated grand administrators and commandants—killed two-thousand-bushel officials—issued proclamations ordering counties hurry prepare food;
translations.idiomatic: Thousand-strong hosts seized towns, looted arsenals, freed felons, trussed governors, slew ranking officials, and demanded grain by manifesto;

sentences.zh: 小群以百數，掠鹵鄉里者不可稱數。

translations.literal: small bands numbered by hundreds—plundering hamlets beyond counting.
translations.idiomatic: smaller gangs in the hundreds pillaged villages without number.

sentences.zh: 於是上始使御史中丞、丞相長史使督之，猶弗能禁，乃使光祿大夫范昆、諸部都尉及故九卿張德等衣繡衣，持節、虎符，發兵以興擊，斬首大部或至萬餘級。

translations.literal: Thereupon the sovereign first sent palace clerks and chancellor chief clerks to supervise—still could not forbid—then sent Superintendent Fan Kun, departmental commandants, and former Nine Ministers Zhang De robed in embroidered coats—bearing credentials and tiger tallies—mobilizing troops to strike—decapitated great bands sometimes exceeding ten thousand heads.
translations.idiomatic: Embroidered inspectors with imperial seals led armies that piled ten thousand heads from the largest bands.

sentences.zh: 及以法誅通行飲食，坐相連郡，甚者數千人。

translations.literal: Moreover by law executed those who supplied passage and food—linked guilt across commanderies—severe cases several thousand persons.
translations.idiomatic: Hosts who fed or sheltered rebels died by statute in linked prosecutions costing thousands of lives.

sentences.zh: 數歲，乃頗得其渠率。

translations.literal: After several years—then somewhat captured their chieftains.
translations.idiomatic: Years passed before ringleaders began to fall.

sentences.zh: 散卒失亡，復聚黨阻山川，往往而群，無可奈何。

translations.literal: Scattered troops lost or fled—regathered bands blocking mountains and rivers—often forming groups—no remedy.
translations.idiomatic: Survivors slipped into mountain holds and regrouped faster than armies could pin them.

sentences.zh: 於是作沈命法，曰：「群盜起不發覺，發覺而弗捕滿品者，二千石以下至小吏主者皆死。

translations.literal: Thereupon enacted Sinking Mandate Law saying: "When bandit gangs arise undetected—or detected but uncaptured below quota—from two-thousand-bushel downward to petty clerks in charge—all die."
translations.idiomatic: The "drowning fate" edict threatened death for every official who missed quota captures.

sentences.zh: 」其後小吏畏誅，雖有盜弗敢發，恐不能得，坐課累府，府亦使不言。

translations.literal: "Afterward petty clerks feared execution—though robbers existed dared not report—feared unable to catch—sat evaluations dragging prefectures—prefecture likewise ordered silence.
translations.idiomatic: Petty officers hid reports rather than fail quotas, and prefects colluded in silence.

sentences.zh: 故盜賊浸多，上下相為匿，以避文法焉。

translations.literal: Therefore robbers-thieves gradually multiplied—high and low mutually concealed—to dodge literary law.
translations.idiomatic: Banditry exploded while bureaucracy masked the truth to survive paperwork.

sentences.zh: 田廣明

translations.literal: Tian Guangming.
translations.idiomatic: Tian Guangming.

sentences.zh: 田廣明字子公，鄭人也。

translations.literal: Tian Guangming styled Zigong—was a man of Zheng.
translations.idiomatic: Tian Guangming, courtesy name Zigong, came from Zheng.

sentences.zh: 以郎為天水司馬。

translations.literal: As gentleman became Tianshui marshal.
translations.idiomatic: He served as marshal of Tianshui from the gentleman corps.

sentences.zh: 攻次遷河南都尉，以殺伐為治。

translations.literal: Merit promoted Hedong commandant—took slaughter as governance.
translations.idiomatic: Merit promotion brought him to Hedong commandant with a slash-and-burn style.

sentences.zh: 郡國盜賊並起，遷廣明為淮陽太守。

translations.literal: Commanderies and kingdoms bandits arose together—promoted Guangming Huaiyang grand administrator.
translations.idiomatic: When revolt spread, he became governor of Huaiyang.

sentences.zh: 歲餘，故城父令公孫勇與客胡倩等謀反，倩詐稱光祿大夫，從車騎數十，言使督盜賊，止陳留傳捨，太守謁見，欲收取之。

translations.literal: After slightly more than a year—former chief of Fufeng Gongsun Yong with guest Hu Qian and others plotted rebellion—Qian falsely claimed superintendent of palace gentlemen—with several tens chariot horses—declared mission supervising robbers—stopped Chenliu relay lodge—grand administrator visited wishing to seize.
translations.idiomatic: Former magistrate Gongsun Yong and Hu Qian forged credentials as an imperial inspector at Chenliu until the local governor moved to arrest them.

sentences.zh: 廣明覺知，發兵皆捕斬焉。

translations.literal: Guangming perceived—mobilized troops—all captured executed.
translations.idiomatic: Tian Guangming uncovered the plot and executed every conspirator.

sentences.zh: 而公孫勇衣繡衣，乘駟馬車至圉，圉使小史侍之，亦知其非是，守尉魏不害與廄嗇夫江德、尉史蘇昌共收捕之。

translations.literal: Meanwhile Gongsun Yong wore embroidered robes—rode four-horse carriage arrived Yu—Yu ordered petty clerk attend—also knew he was false—guard commandant Wei Buhai with stable bailiff Jiang De and clerk Su Chang together seized.
translations.idiomatic: Gongsun Yong rode into Yu in counterfeit splendor until petty clerks and Commander Wei Buhai arrested him with Jiang De and Su Chang.

sentences.zh: 上封不害為當塗侯，德為陽侯，昌蒲侯。

translations.literal: Sovereign enfeoffed Buhai as Dangtu marquis—De as Yang marquis—Chang as Pu marquis.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor made Wei Buhai marquis of Dangtu, Jiang De marquis of Yang, and Su Chang marquis of Pu.

sentences.zh: 初，四人俱拜於前，小史竊言。

translations.literal: Formerly four men together bowed forward—petty clerk whispered.
translations.idiomatic: At the mass investiture a junior clerk muttered aside.

sentences.zh: 武帝問：「言何？

translations.literal: The emperor asked: "What did he say?"
translations.idiomatic: The emperor asked what the whisper meant.

sentences.zh: 」對曰：「為侯者得東歸不？

translations.literal: He replied: "May one enfeoffed as marquis return east?"
translations.idiomatic: He whispered whether new marquises were allowed to travel home east of the mountains.

sentences.zh: 」上曰：「女欲不？

translations.literal: The emperor said: "Do you not wish to be ennobled?"
translations.idiomatic: The emperor asked, "Would you rather not take the honor?"

sentences.zh: 貴矣。

translations.literal: You are ennobled already."
translations.idiomatic: You are a noble now."

sentences.zh: 女鄉名為何？

translations.literal: What is the name of your home village?"
translations.idiomatic: What is your native village called?"

sentences.zh: 」對曰：「名遺鄉。

translations.literal: He replied: "It is called the Left-Behind Hamlet."
translations.idiomatic: He said it was called Yixiang, 'the Hamlet of the Left-Behind.'"

sentences.zh: 」上曰：「用遺汝矣。

translations.literal: The emperor said: "I bequeath that name to you."
translations.idiomatic: The emperor answered, "Then the 'left-behind' fief is yours."

sentences.zh: 」於是賜小史爵關內侯，食遺鄉六百戶。

translations.literal: Thereupon he granted the petty clerk the rank of marquis within the passes with six hundred households at Yixiang.
translations.idiomatic: He ennobled the clerk as marquis within the passes with six hundred households at Yixiang.

sentences.zh: 上以廣明連禽大奸，征入為大鴻臚，擢廣明兄雲中代為淮陽太守。

translations.literal: Sovereign because Guangming repeatedly captured great villains—summoned as grand herald—promoted Guangming's elder brother Yunnan in Yunzhong as substitute Huaiyang grand administrator.
translations.idiomatic: For crushing major plots he entered the capital as grand herald while his brother governed Huaiyang.

sentences.zh: 昭帝時，廣明將兵擊益州，還，賜爵關內侯，徙衛尉。

translations.literal: In Emperor Zhao's time Guangming led troops striking Yizhou—returned—granted noble rank marquis within passes—transferred guard commandant.
translations.idiomatic: Under Emperor Zhao he campaigned in Yizhou, earned a marquisette, and became guard commandant.

sentences.zh: 後出為左馮翊，治有能名。

translations.literal: Later went out as Zuo Fengyi—governance had capable name.
translations.idiomatic: Later he governed Zuo Fengyi with a reputation for competence.

sentences.zh: 宣帝初立，代蔡義為御史大夫，以前為馮翊與議定策，封昌水侯。

translations.literal: When Emperor Xuande first rose—replaced Cai Yi as imperial clerk—because earlier as Fengyi participated settling succession policy—enfeoffed Changshui marquis.
translations.idiomatic: Emperor Xuan named him imperial clerk for aiding the succession and created him marquis of Changshui.

sentences.zh: 歲餘，以祁連將軍將兵擊匈奴，出塞至受降城。

translations.literal: After slightly more than year—as Qilian general led troops striking Xiongnu—exited frontier reaching Surrender Accept city.
translations.idiomatic: As Qilian general he marched to Accept Surrender City beyond the wall.

sentences.zh: 受降都尉前死，喪柩在堂，廣明召其寡妻與奸。

translations.literal: Surrender Accept commandant died earlier—mourning coffin in hall—Guangming summoned his widow and committed adultery.
translations.idiomatic: He seduced the widow of the late surrender commandant while her husband's coffin still lay in state.

sentences.zh: 既出不至質，引軍空還。

translations.literal: Already exited—not reaching hostage rendezvous—led army empty return.
translations.idiomatic: He halted short of the hostage exchange and marched home empty-handed.

sentences.zh: 下太僕杜延年簿責，廣明自殺闕下，國除。

translations.literal: Ordered Grand Coachman Du Yanian register reprimand—Guangming killed himself below palace gate—state abolished.
translations.idiomatic: Grand Coachman Du Yanian's audit drove him to suicide at the palace stairs and forfeiture of his fief.

sentences.zh: 兄雲中為淮陽守，亦敢誅殺，吏民守闕告之，竟坐棄市。

translations.literal: Elder brother Yunnan as Huaiyang guard—also dared execute kill—clerks and people guarded palace gate reporting—finally sentenced exposed market.
translations.idiomatic: His brother Yunnan, another bloody governor of Huaiyang, fell to petitions at the palace gate and died in the marketplace.

sentences.zh: 田延年

translations.literal: Tian Yannian.
translations.idiomatic: Tian Yannian.

sentences.zh: 先是，茂陵富人焦氏、賈氏以數千萬陰積貯炭葦諸下里物。

translations.literal: Formerly Maoling magnates the Jiao and Jia families hoarded charcoal, reeds, and other burial supplies worth tens of millions in secret.
translations.idiomatic: Maoling merchants Jiao and Jia had secretly stockpiled charcoal, reed matting, and other tomb supplies worth tens of millions.

sentences.zh: 昭帝大行時，方上事暴起，用度未辦，延年奏言：「商賈或豫收方上不祥器物，冀其疾用，欲以求利，非民臣所當為。

translations.literal: When Emperor Zhao lay in state, tomb works suddenly surged and funds were short—Yannian memorialized: "Some merchants have hoarded ill-omened grave goods beforehand, hoping for urgent demand to seek profit—this is not what subjects ought do."
translations.idiomatic: During Emperor Zhao's lying-in-state the imperial tomb project exploded overnight without funds ready; Tian Yannian accused merchants of hoarding funeral supplies for profit.

sentences.zh: 請沒入縣官。

translations.literal: Request confiscate to county offices.
translations.idiomatic: He asked to seize their hoards for the treasury.

sentences.zh: 」奏可。

translations.literal: The memorial was approved.
translations.idiomatic: The throne approved his request.

sentences.zh: 富人亡財者皆怨，出錢求延年罪。

translations.literal: Wealthy who lost fortune all resented—produced cash seeking Yannian crimes.
translations.idiomatic: Ruined plutocrats pooled silver hunting his scalp.

sentences.zh: 初，大司農取民牛車三萬兩為僦，載沙便橋下，送致方上，車直千錢，延年上簿詐增僦直車二千，凡六千萬，盜取其半。

translations.literal: Formerly Excellency of Agriculture took people's ox carts thirty thousand pairs as hire—carried sand beneath Bian Bridge—delivered to tomb mound—cart worth thousand cash—Yannian upward register falsely increased hire worth carts two thousand—total sixty million—stole half.
translations.idiomatic: As Excellency of Agriculture he inflated ox-cart hire from one thousand to three thousand cash per cart on thirty thousand vehicles—embezzelling thirty million.

sentences.zh: 焦、賈兩家告其事，下丞相府。

translations.literal: Jiao and Jia two houses reported affair—sent down chancellor bureau.
translations.idiomatic: The Jiao and Jia families exposed him to the chancellor.

sentences.zh: 丞相議奏延年「主守盜三千萬，不道」。

translations.literal: Chancellor deliberated memorial Yannian "chief guarding theft thirty million—not Way".
translations.idiomatic: The chancellor charged him with thirty million in peculation.

sentences.zh: 霍將軍召問延年，欲為道地，延年抵曰：「本出將軍之門，蒙此爵位，無有是事。

translations.literal: General Huo summoned Tian Yannian for questioning and wished to smooth things over; Yannian denied it, saying: "I rose from your household and owe these ranks—I did no such thing."
translations.idiomatic: Huo Guang questioned him; Tian Yannian insisted he was innocent despite owing everything to the general.

sentences.zh: 」光曰：「即無事，當窮竟。

translations.literal: Guang said: "If there is nothing to it, it must be fully investigated."
translations.idiomatic: Huo Guang answered, "If you are blameless, submit to a full inquiry."

sentences.zh: 」御史大夫田廣明謂太僕杜延年：「《春秋》之義，以功覆過。

translations.literal: Imperial Clerk Tian Guangming said to Grand Coachman Du Yanian: "By the principle of the Spring and Autumn Annals, merit may cover fault."
translations.idiomatic: Imperial Clerk Tian Guangming urged Du Yanian that merit might excuse guilt under Spring and Autumn precedent.

sentences.zh: 當廢昌邑王時，非田子賓之言大事不成。

translations.literal: When we deposed the Prince of Changyi, without Tian Zibin's counsel the great deed would not have succeeded."
translations.idiomatic: Without Tian Yannin's advice we could never have deposed Prince He of Changyi."

sentences.zh: 今縣官出三千萬自乞之何哉？

translations.literal: Now county offices produce thirty million begging it—what?
translations.idiomatic: Why should the court spend thirty million buying his life?"

sentences.zh: 願以愚言白大將軍。

translations.literal: Wish by foolish words inform grand general.
translations.idiomatic: Let me speak to the general for you."

sentences.zh: 」延年言之大將軍，大將軍曰：「誠然，實勇士也！

translations.literal: Du Yannian relayed this to the Grand General; the Grand General said: "True enough—he was a fearless man!
translations.idiomatic: Du Yannian carried the plea to Huo Guang, who replied: "So he was—the bravest voice in the hall when we debated deposition."

sentences.zh: 當發大議時，震動朝廷。

translations.literal: When issuing great deliberation—shook entire court.
translations.idiomatic: His speech shook the whole court."

sentences.zh: 」光因舉手自撫心曰：「使我至今病悸！

translations.literal: Guang therefore raised his hand to his breast and said: "That speech still leaves my heart pounding!
translations.idiomatic: Huo Guang pressed his palm to his chest: "Even now recalling it sets my heart racing!"

sentences.zh: 謝田大夫曉大司農，通往就獄，得公議之。

translations.literal: Ask Minister Tian to instruct the Excellency of Agriculture to give himself up and enter prison so the matter may be judged openly.
translations.idiomatic: Tell Tian Guangming to urge Tian Yannian to surrender so the matter may be tried openly."

sentences.zh: 」田大夫使人語延年，延年曰：「幸縣官寬我耳，何面目入牢獄，使眾人指笑我，卒徒唾吾背乎！

translations.literal: Minister Tian sent someone to speak to Tian Yannian; Tian Yannian said: "If the court only pardons me—what face would I have entering a dungeon while folk point and laugh and guards spit on my back!
translations.idiomatic: When Tian Guangming's messenger pressed him to surrender, Tian Yannian refused to die a prisoner mocked by the mob.

sentences.zh: 」即閉閣獨居齊捨，偏袒持刀東西步。

translations.literal: He immediately barred his doors and shut himself in his quarters—bare-shouldered, gripping a knife, pacing back and forth.
translations.idiomatic: He locked himself in, bared one shoulder, clutched a knife, and paced.

sentences.zh: 數日，使者召延年詣廷尉。

translations.literal: Several days—envoy summoned Yannian go commandant of justice.
translations.idiomatic: Days later the summons to the commandant of justice arrived.

sentences.zh: 聞鼓聲，自刎死，國除。

translations.literal: Hearing drum sound—cut throat died—state abolished.
translations.idiomatic: At the signal drum he cut his throat and lost his fief.

sentences.zh: 嚴延年

translations.literal: Yan Yannian.
translations.idiomatic: Yan Yannian.

sentences.zh: 嚴延年字次卿，東海下邳人也。

translations.literal: Yan Yannian styled Ciqing—was a man of Xiapi in Donghai.
translations.idiomatic: Yan Yannian, courtesy name Ciqing, came from Xiapi in Donghai.

sentences.zh: 其父為丞相掾，延年少學法律丞相府，歸為郡吏。

translations.literal: His father was chancellor clerk—Yannian youth studied law in chancellor bureau—returned county clerk.
translations.idiomatic: His father served the chancellor; he studied law there and became a county clerk.

sentences.zh: 以選除補御史掾，舉侍御史。

translations.literal: By selection removed supplemented clerk to censor—raised attendant censor.
translations.idiomatic: Selection moved him to the censorate and promotion made him attendant censor.

sentences.zh: 是時，大將軍霍光廢昌邑王，尊立宣帝。

translations.literal: At this time Grand General Huo Guang deposed Prince He of Changyi—raised established Emperor Xuan.
translations.idiomatic: When Huo Guang deposed Liu He and raised Emperor Xuan,

sentences.zh: 宣帝初即位，延年劾奏光「擅廢立主，無人臣禮，不道」。

translations.literal: Emperor Xuan first ascended—Yannian impeachment memorial Huo Guang "arbitrarily deposing enthroning lord—lacking minister ritual—not Way".
translations.idiomatic: Yan Yannian indicted Huo Guang for treasonous arrogation.

sentences.zh: 奏雖寢，然朝廷肅焉敬憚。

translations.literal: Memorial though shelved—yet court soberly respected feared.
translations.idiomatic: The memorial died in committee, yet the court stood in awe.

sentences.zh: 延年後復劾大司農田延年持兵干屬車，大司農自訟不干屬車。

translations.literal: Yannian later again impeached Excellency of Agriculture Tian Yannian bearing weapons interfering escort carriage—Excellency of Agriculture self-litigated not interfering escort carriage.
translations.idiomatic: He later charged Tian Yannian with brandishing arms near the imperial escort—countered by Tian's denial.

sentences.zh: 事下御史中丞，譴責延年何以不移書宮殿門禁止大司農，而令得出入宮。

translations.literal: Affair sent palace clerk—reprimanded Yannian why not transfer document palace gate forbidding Excellency of Agriculture—allowing coming going palace.
translations.idiomatic: The vice-censor rebuked Yan for failing to seal the palace gate against the minister.

sentences.zh: 於是復劾延年闌內罪人，法至死。

translations.literal: Thereupon again impeached Yannian bursting gate inner criminal—law reaching death.
translations.idiomatic: He then accused Tian of smuggling felons inside—a capital crime.

sentences.zh: 延年亡命。

translations.literal: Yannian fled life.
translations.idiomatic: Tian Yannian fled.

sentences.zh: 會赦出，丞相、御史府征書同日到，延年以御史書先至，詣御史府，復為掾。

translations.literal: Met amnesty released—chancellor and censor offices summons arrived same day—Yannian because censor document arrived first—went censor bureau—again clerk.
